utterances: Political Junkie Ken Rudin with us here in Studio 3A.
You can read his blog and find his ScuttleButton puzzle at npr.org/junkie.
And, Ken, of course, we'll see you next week.
Tomorrow, after the latest nuclear watchdog report on Iran, what now?
We end with the music that we use every week for the Political Junkie.
It's maybe not appropriate, you would think, after we just mentioned the death of somebody like Hal Bruno.
Hal Bruno would like this music.
Let's bring it up.
I'm Neal Conan.
It's the TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
Afghanistan voted over the weekend - or most of it did.
The killings of top officials in one major province forced a delay there.
But the rest of the country cast ballots for Parliament.
Pamela Constable of The Washington Post joined us on Friday just before the voting, and now she's back.
Hi, Pam.
Hi, how are you?
OK.
Thank you very much.
What was voting day like?
Well, it was pretty chaotic.

Every polling place I visited in the capital and dozens of polling places across the country reported large problems with disorganization, unprepared polling officials, lost materials, biometric equipment that didn't work, very, very, very long lines.
Many of the polls opened three, four, five hours late.
Some did not open at all.
So it was a well-intentioned effort, but the practical results were pretty chaotic.
Is it clear that enough ballots were cast in a legitimate way that this will feel like a legitimate result?

That's a good question, and I can't answer it yet because as you mentioned, you know, a large number of voters in one province, Kandahar, where there were some serious killings in the past few days, has - have been delayed for one week.
And in a second province, Ghazni, where there have been recent attacks as well, that voting has also been delayed.

So you've got two provinces where the voting hasn't even happened yet, and you also have dozens of - possibly even hundreds of polling stations both in Kabul and elsewhere where people had to vote yesterday - a second day of voting because the polls never opened on election day itself.
And election officials say there are not going to be any results of any significance for weeks and possibly even for a couple of months.
Wow.
So we have no idea who won these elections and - given that they're not even quite over.
But let me ask you about what the winners will face.
We should note people know from a distance that Afghanistan has had a very powerful president to the extent that it's had a central government at all.

And of course a lot of decisions in some cases are in the hands or influenced by the United States, influenced by allies or, for that matter, influenced by the Taliban, which controls portions of the country.
Will this parliament, once it's seated, have much to say?
What will their role be?
That's also a good question.
I think and I hope that the country is in for a change.

When I was reporting from the campaign, interviewing candidates and interviewing voters, I found an enormous amount of - a surprisingly large amount of enthusiasm and hope that this election actually would bring change in the parliament itself - young people voting, young people running, more educated people voting and running and a lot of hope to sort of - out with the old faces, in with the new.
So I think there is still hope for that.
Pamela Constable of The Washington Post, always appreciate your insights.
Thanks so much.
You're very welcome.
She's in Kabul.
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
Barack Obama ran for president on a promise to focus on the war in Afghanistan and initiated a policy review as soon as he took office, then another a few months later.
He's due to receive yet another, later this week.
Over almost two years in office, the president's escalated U.S.
troop levels twice, fired two commanders and extended the timeline for U.S.
combat operations to 2014, a process documented by veteran journalist Bob Woodward in his most recent book.
Obama's Wars"" describes White House and military officials determined to get it right amid mutual distrust and sometimes mutual incomprehension.
If you've read the book, has your opinion of the president changed?
Our phone number is 800-989-8255.
Email us, talk@npr.org.
You can also join the conversation at our website.
That's at npr.org.
Just click on TALK OF THE NATION.

Before we begin, a major player in the discussion, Richard Holbrooke, the special representative for Afghanistan and Pakistan, is reported in critical condition following heart surgery at a hospital in Washington.
We wish him well.
Bob Woodward joins us in Studio 3A.
Nice to have you back.
A little bit later, we'll be talking about technology in the coming year, and also toxins in the home.
But up first - speaking of toxins, coral reefs from Australia's Great Barrier to reefs in the Caribbean are heading for extinction.

If you want your grandchildren to swim with the pretty fishes and among the coral heads, you'd better have them do it really soon because they're going to be gone - history, in the textbooks, killed off - within decades.
That's the gist of the bad news in a new study out today in the journal Science that looks at how climate change will change the ocean, and one ocean ecosystem in particular: coal reefs.
And it's very blunt.
If global warming continues at its present rate, the world's coral reefs will be gone by mid-century.
Joining us now from the fall meeting of the American Geophysical Union in San Francisco, where he's presenting these findings, is one of the study's authors.
Ken Caldeira is a chemical oceanographer at Carnegie Institution's Department of Global Ecology at Stanford University.
Welcome to the program.
Thank you for inviting me to be on.
Did I overstate the case?

I - maybe you slightly did in a sense that you're correct that if carbon dioxide emissions continue according to current trends, by mid-century, conditions will be such that coral reefs won't be able to survive in them.
It might take a little more time for them to actually be killed off, but the conditions that would allow them to live sustainably will be gone.
In your promo leading in before the news, you basically - you said that it may be already be too late for the coral reefs.
And I don't think that's the case.
I think if we dramatically reduce carbon dioxide emissions soon that they can still be saved.

But this is intended to be a wake-up call; that we really need to do something now to reduce emissions if we want these communities that have lived for many, many, millions of years to continue existing on our planet.
But let's look at this a little bit closer.
Let's talk about why the coral reefs are dying from CO2 emissions.
There…
Tell us what the connection is.
Okay, there are two different connections.
Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas and has a climate effect, and that warming of the ocean surface, the heating up of the waters, stresses coral reefs and makes it hard for them to grow.
But perhaps more insidious effect is that carbon dioxide, once it dissolves in the ocean, becomes carbonic acid.
And this attacks the shells and skeletons of marine organisms, including corals, and makes it more difficult for them to build their skeletons.
When they're weakened by the heating and the acidification, they can no longer compete ecologically, and eventually algaes in sea grasses will take over.
Mm-hmm.
And at what level of parts per million of CO2 are we now and where does the danger happen?
Right now, we're around 380 parts per million - that's about a hundred higher than it was before the industrial revolution - that by mid-century or so, we're expected to reach 500 parts per million.
And around that time, the kind of water where corals were able to prevail before the industrial revolution that none of that kind of water will be left in the ocean.
The ocean has a chemical force that helps coral reefs build their skeletons.
And when their chemical force is too weak, the corals can't build their skeletons fast enough to compete.
And basically, by mid-century, there'll be no place left in the ocean with enough of that chemical force to help corals build their skeletons.
So they would all die out.
They will all die out.
They might not die all of a sudden.
You can actually grow corals in a fish tank, in waters that are less conducive to skeletal growth.
But what happens in the real world is maybe there's a storm or something, it's some kind of disturbance, and then it comes time to reoccupy that territory.
And if the corals are weakened, it will be the sea grasses or the algaes that will reoccupy the territory and not the coral reef.
And so they might hang on a little bit longer in certain places, but there'll be no place left in the ocean where they'll be able to compete successfully over the long term.
If we were to implement the Kyoto treaty or all these other treaties that we're talking about and reduce our levels of CO2, at what level would the ocean stabilize from this point?

The kinds of treaties that people are looking at now would stabilize - would result into stabilization above the 500 level that we're talking about and therefore would not be enough to save the coral reefs.
I think if we are serious about saving the coral reefs, we really need to think about not, say, slight improvements in the CAFE standards for automobiles and things like that.
But we have to think, well, a decade from now, we can no longer be making gasoline-powered cars and we have to transition over the next decade to some other kind of transportation system.
It's those kind of radical, fundamental restructuring of our energy system that we would need very rapidly if we want to save the reef.
Do you think that's possible, practically speaking?

I think it's certainly possible in the same sense that when we were confronted by the threat of World War II, we went from biplanes to jet airplanes and developed integrated circuits and radar and all kinds of other technologies in just a few years.
But the important thing was seeing a big threat and responding to it.
To me, the coral reefs, an important as they are, are a bit like the bird in the coal mine, the canary in the coal mine.

And if these big, giant, beautiful structures that have been around for many, many tens of millions of years are disappearing as a result of what we're doing in a few decades, that's a sign that we're disturbing the way the Earth is functioning in some very fundamental ways.
And this should serve as a wake-up call that we really need to address this problem in a fundamental way and respond to it in the same kind of urgency that we respond to military threats.

If we're having trouble just getting everybody to sign on Kyoto and get down to a level that they want of 5,000 parts per million - and you say we have to even go lower than if everybody signed on level to save the corals - that's why I - it's says to me there really is not much chance that they're going to be around much longer.
You know, it's very difficult to predict social and political systems.
Nobody foresaw the demise of the Soviet Union just even a year or two before it collapsed.

And I think it is possible - and maybe it's unlikely - but we could wake up and say that we want to have a planet available for future generations that is somehow resembles the kind of planet our fathers and grandfathers grew up in.
And, you know, if we come to that realization, we can solve this problem.
It's not that hard a problem.
It just takes political will and the creativity of people to find good, affordable solutions.
I'm sure those exist.

And - but I think when we see the ice caps melting and ice sheets collapsing and the coral reefs disappearing, that's - those kinds of things should serve as a wake-up call that we're doing something seriously wrong and that we can't continue using the atmosphere and the oceans as a dumping ground.
But that's what…
So, what's…
…but that's one of the proposals, is it not, to take some of the CO2 that we're pumping into the atmosphere and stick it in the oceans?
Yes.
I think most scientists who have looked at that no longer think that's a particularly good idea.
The ocean ecosystems respond very sensitively to added carbon dioxide.
It's an acid in sea water and it's attacking the shells of marine organisms.
We're focusing here on corals, but here are many, many other organisms like clams and snails and so on that makes shells out of calcium carbonate.
And by the middle of the century, if current emission trends continue, some of those shells will start dissolving, especially in polar water.
